text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
We have spent several studies looking carefully at the Table of Showbread; its names, its construction, the twelve loaves of fine flour, the frankincense, and how every detail pointed precisely to Christ. Now we come to the question we have been building toward. The Kohathites carried this table on their shoulders through the wilderness. We are the new covenant Kohathites; the assembled, called-together people of God. So, what are we carrying on our shoulders today? What does the table look like when it is carried by a new creation believer in the twenty-first century? The answer is more personal than we might expect. Because the table we carry is not outside us. It is us.

The Kohathite did not carry the table in his hands at arm’s length. It rested on his shoulder; pressed against his body, his neck feeling the weight, his back bearing the load step by step. This is how God has chosen for His holy things to move through the world today. Not carried from a distance. Not managed at arm’s length. Pressed against our lives, formed into our character, borne through every step of our wilderness journey. Let us look at what we are actually carrying.

The table was built from acacia wood; sittim wood the wood that does not rot in the desert. We saw that this wood pointed to the incorruptible character of Christ as a man. Now here is the searching question: when we became new creations in Christ, what happened to us? 2 Corinthians 5:17 says: “If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come.” The new creation is not a repaired version of the old nature. It is not patched-up acacia wood. It is entirely new wood; a new nature, created in true righteousness and holiness (Ephesians 4:24), incorruptible in its origin because it comes from God Himself.

1 Peter 1:23 says we have been “born again, not of perishable seed but of imperishable.” The old nature was perishable. It decayed under pressure, rotted under temptation, warped under hardship. But the new creation has been given an incorruptible seed. The sittim wood is in us now. We carry it on our shoulders into the world every time we walk through a situation that would have destroyed the old nature and find that something in us holds. When we refuse to compromise where we once would have collapsed. When we stand where we once fell. When we endure what the old man could never have endured. That is the sittim wood carrying us,
even as we carry it.

But here is the weight of this. The acacia tree grew in the desert; in hard conditions, in dry ground, under unforgiving heat. The incorruptible nature in us is not proved in comfortable conditions. It is proved in the wilderness. It is proved when life is dry, when God feels far, when the journey is long, when nobody is watching. God is watching every new creation believers in their private wilderness to see if the wood holds. And when it holds; when a believer lives with integrity in hardship, when they maintain their character in the desert seasons of life; they are carrying the table. They are showing the world what incorruptible wood looks like in a world that rots.

The table was wood covered with gold; inside and outside. We saw that this points to the character of Christ, in whom the fullness of what belongs to God dwells (Colossians 2:9). But now consider what Paul says in 2 Corinthians 3:18: “We all,
with unveiled face, beholding the glory of Yahweh, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another.” This is the gold being laid on the wood. We are not born again already fully gold. We are born again with sittim wood; the new nature and then we are being covered, layer by layer, through a lifetime of transformation into the image of Yahweh.

This is one of the most sobering truths in Scripture. The gold was not painted onto the table in one day. Beaten sheets of pure gold were carefully overlaid across every surface; every corner, every curve, every part of the inside as well as the outside. There was no quick process. And transformation into the character of Christ is not quick.
It is the patient, daily, sometimes painful work of God covering our old edges, our rough corners, our hidden parts, with the gold of His character. The inside matters as much as the outside. The gold was on the inside of the table where no one could see it. God is working on the parts of us that no one can see; the private motives, the hidden attitudes, the secret thoughts covering those as carefully as the visible parts.

And here is what this means for the carrying. The more we are being transformed into the character of Christ; the more we yield to the process, the more we allow the Word and the Spirit to cover the rough places in us, the more we are able to carry what God has placed on our shoulders. A table with cracked, uncoated wood would not have been fit for the Holy Place. A believer who resists transformation, who protects their rough edges and refuses the work of God on the inside, carries the table poorly.
The gold is not just for our benefit. It is what makes us fit to carry what God has entrusted to us.

The table wore a double crown of gold around its rim. We saw that this crown pointed to Christ crowned with glory and honour (Hebrews 2:9), and that the crown practically kept the bread from falling off during the journey.
Now consider what James 1:12 says: “Blessed is the man who remains steadfast under trial, for when he has stood the test he will receive the crown of life, which God has promised to those who love Him.” And Revelation 2:10: “Be faithful unto death, and I will give you the crown of life.”

We carry a crown. Not yet fully received, but already promised and already resting on our identity as the people of the King. We walk through this world as crowned people; not crowned by the world’s standards, not recognised or honoured by the systems around us, but crowned in the sight of God. This changes how we carry everything else. A person who knows they are crowned does not beg for the approval of the world. A person who knows they are destined for the crown of life does not trade that crown for a moment of compromise. And just as the crown on the table kept the bread from falling off during the wilderness journey, the promise of the crown keeps us from falling away during our own wilderness. We carry the crown of life on our shoulders, and the crown holds us in place.

But we have only seen part of what is on our shoulders. The table also carried twelve loaves of fine flour; unleavened, equal, and always before the face of God. And it carried frankincense rising as a fragrance before Yahweh. What do these mean for us as new creation believers today? That is where we will continue tomorrow. For now, let this settle in the heart: we are the table. The wood, the gold, the crown; these are not things we hold in our hands. They are what we are being shaped into, day by day, through the wilderness. And what we are shapes what we carry.

title: Prayer
text:
Yahweh, You are the one who forms and transforms. You took sittim wood and made a table fit for Your presence. You are taking us; new creations born of imperishable seed and covering us with the gold of Your character, layer by layer, from the inside out.

We confess that we have sometimes resisted the process. We have pulled back from the places where the gold was being laid because it was uncomfortable. Forgive us. Help us to yield to the transformation. Help us to allow You to cover the hidden corners, the inside surfaces, the parts of our lives that no one sees but You. And help us to carry the crown of life with the dignity it deserves; not trading it for the applause of the world, not dropping it under the pressure of the wilderness.

May what we carry on our shoulders today reflect what You are making us to be. In Christ’s name, amen.
